chunks.text: Dictionaries and Their Implementations Chapter 18 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Dictionary FIGURE 18-1 A collection of data about certain cities © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Consider need to search such a collection for – Name – Address – Population by City, E.g. population of London • Binary search on City (sorted) – Population of cities in Spain • Scan entire collection • Criterion chosen for search is search key • The ADT dictionary uses a search key to identify its entries The ADT Dictionary FIGURE 18-2 UML diagram for a class of dictionaries © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Possible Implementations • Categories of linear implementations – Sorted by search key array-based – Sorted by search key link-based – Unsorted array-based – Unsorted link-based © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 18-3 A dictionary entry Search key Data Value insert a new item into proper position based on the value of search key insert a new item into any convenient location Figure 18-4 Data members for two sorted linear implementations of the ADT dictionary for the data in Figure 18-1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Possible Implementations • A linear array-based implementation of the ADT dictionary must shift data during an insertion or deletion in sorted order, which can be expensive, particularly for large dictionaries. • A linear link-based implementation of the ADT dictionary eliminates the need to shift data, it does not support the insertion or removal operations any more efficiently than an array-based implementation, because you cannot perform a binary search in a reasonable fashion. FIGURE 18-5 The data members for a binary search tree implementation of the ADT dictionary for the data in Figure 18-1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Possible Implementations Another option is implementing it as a binary search tree. Selecting an Implementation • Linear implementations – Perspective – Efficiency – Motivation • Consider – What operations are needed – How often each operation is required © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Three Scenarios A. Addition and traversal in no particular order – Unsorted order is efficient – Array-based versus pointer-based B. Retrieval – Sorted array-based can use binary search – Binary search impractical for link-based – Max size of dictionary affects choice C. Addition, removal, retrieval, traversal in sorted order – Add and remove need to find position, then add or remove from that position – Array-based best for find, link-based best for addition/removal © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Three Scenarios FIGURE 18-6 Addition for unsorted linear implementations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Three Scenarios FIGURE 18-7 Addition for sorted linear implementations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Three Scenarios FIGURE 18-8 The average-case order of the ADT dictionary operations for various implementations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Situations occur for which search-tree implementations are not adequate. • Consider a method which acts as an “address calculator” which determines an array index – Used for add, getValue, remove operations • Called a hash function – Tells where to place item in a hash table

NJ. All rights reserved Three Scenarios FIGURE 18-8 The average-case order of the ADT dictionary operations for various implementations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Situations occur for which search-tree implementations are not adequate. • Consider a method which acts as an “address calculator” which determines an array index – Used for add, getValue, remove operations • Called a hash function – Tells where to place item in a hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation FIGURE 18-9 Address calculator © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Perfect hash function – Maps each search key into a unique location of the hash table – Possible if you know all the search keys • Collision occurs when hash function maps more than one entry into same array location • Hash function should – Be easy, fast to compute – Place entries evenly throughout hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Hash Functions • Sufficient for hash functions to operate on integers – examples: – Select digits from an ID number • Generally uneven distribution – Folding – add digits, sum is the table location – Modulo arithmetic h(x) = x mod tableSize • Simple, effective • Use prime number for table size – Convert character string to an integer – use ASCII values • Address potential overflow © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • Approach 1: Open addressing – Linear probing – Quadratic probing – Double hashing – Increase size of hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-10 A collision © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-11 Linear probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Linear probing • Add - search the hash table, starting from the original hash location; if table[h(searchKey)] is occupied, check table[h(searchKey)+1] , table[h(searchKey)+2], … until you find an available location. • Delete –place table location into one of three states: • occupied (currently in use), • empty (has not been used), or • Modify addItem to insert at this location • removed (was once occupied but is now available) • Modify getItem to continue probing • Modify addItem to insert at this location • Items tend to cluster together in the hash table, when using this approach - primary clustering. • Clusters can get close to one another and, merge into a larger cluster. • Large clusters tend to get even larger Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-12 Quadratic probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quadratic probing. • You can virtually eliminate primary clusters by adjusting the linear probing scheme. • When two items hash collide, quadratic probing uses the same probe sequence for each item. • The resulting phenomenon—called secondary clustering —delays the resolution of the collision. • Although the research of quadratic probing remains incomplete, it appears that secondary clustering is not a problem. Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-13 Double hashing during the addition

Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quadratic probing. • You can virtually eliminate primary clusters by adjusting the linear probing scheme. • When two items hash collide, quadratic probing uses the same probe sequence for each item. • The resulting phenomenon—called secondary clustering —delays the resolution of the collision. • Although the research of quadratic probing remains incomplete, it appears that secondary clustering is not a problem. Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-13 Double hashing during the addition of 58, 14, and 91 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Double hashing - defines key-dependent probe sequences. • searches the hash table in a linear order, starting at the location h1(key), • second hash function h2 determines the size of the steps taken • h2(key) <> 0 • h2 <> h1 • For example, let h1 and h2 be the primary and secondary hash functions defined as • h1 ( key ) = key mod 11 // 11 is table size and prime number • h2(key) = 7 (key mod 7) // visits all the table locations (table size and step are prime) Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure • You should not double the size of the array, since the size of the hash table should be prime. • You do not move the items from the original hash table to the new hash table, since your hash function may have been impacted, e.g., x mod tableSize • You need to apply your new hash function to every item in the old hash table before placing it into the new hash table. (Rehashing); expensive • Approach 2: Resolving collisions by restructuring the hash table – Buckets • Define each has table location table[i] as an array, aka, bucket, where you store the collided items into in this array • Too small – only postponed collision problem • Too big – wasted storage – Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure FIGURE 18-14 Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Separate chaining. Design the hash table as an array of linked chains • Each entry table[i] is a pointer to a chain of linked nodes containing the items that the hash function has mapped into location i • The size of the dictionary is dynamic and can exceed the size of the hash table, because each linked chain can be as long as necessary. • The most time-efficient collision-resolution scheme. The Efficiency of Hashing • Load factor measures how full a hash table is • Unsuccessful searches – Generally require more time than successful • Do not let the hash table get too full © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Efficiency of Hashing FIGURE 18-15 The relative efficiency of four collision-resolution methods © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved What Constitutes a Good Hash Function? • Is hash function easy and fast to compute? • Does hash function scatter data evenly throughout hash table? • How well does hash function scatter random data? • How well does hash function scatter non- random data? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Dictionary Traversal: An Inefficient Operation Under Hashing • Entries hashed into table[i] and table[i+1] have no

collision-resolution methods © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved What Constitutes a Good Hash Function? • Is hash function easy and fast to compute? • Does hash function scatter data evenly throughout hash table? • How well does hash function scatter random data? • How well does hash function scatter non- random data? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Dictionary Traversal: An Inefficient Operation Under Hashing • Entries hashed into table[i] and table[i+1] have no ordering relationship • Hashing does not support well traversing a dictionary in sorted order – Generally better to use a search tree • In external storage possible to see – Hashing implementation of getValue – And search-tree for ordered operations simultaneously © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ.
